text.title: Technical SEO for AI Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Technical SEO for AI application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them. Plus a skip-methodology rule for decision-tree leaves that exit the methodology.

rule.statement: Every site ships an `llms.txt` at the root with crawler-allow / disallow rules and a content-license note. Missing file on a public-content site = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Without llms.txt, AI crawlers default to opaque heuristics; signalling intent is the floor.

rule.statement: Every article page implements Article schema with `author`, `datePublished`, `dateModified`, `headline`, and `image`. Missing any field = REJECT.
Author byline carries machine-readable credentials (sameAs to LinkedIn / GitHub / Google Scholar). Anonymous bylines = REJECT for authority pages.
rule.rationale: Author authority signals are how AI ranks trustworthiness.

rule.statement: URL structure is shallow (≤3 segments), human-readable, and matches the breadcrumb hierarchy. Cryptic URLs = REJECT.
rule.rationale: URL structure is a first-pass authority signal.

rule.statement: Core Web Vitals measured on FIELD data (CrUX / PageSpeed Insights real-user) not LAB data. LCP <2.5s, CLS <0.1, INP <200ms. Lab-only measurement = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Lab numbers mislead; AI crawlers prefer fast-loading pages.

rule.statement: Named entities (products, authors, brand) use the same canonical form in schema, body, and breadcrumbs. Drift = REJECT.
rule.rationale: AI crawlers reconcile across sources; drift lowers ranking.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the decision tree resolves to a skip branch — preconditions are not met OR an adjacent methodology fits better.
rule.rationale: Skipping cleanly preserves the integrity of the rubric; misapplied methodologies produce false positives downstream.
